speaker: interviewer
text: --22, 1996. OK. Now you're in Chrzanow right after the war in-- in January 1945. How long did you stay in Chrzanow?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chrzanow. Short. Weeks. Weeks. But first of all, I wanted to see who was coming back. My brother-in-law came back, Jacobovits. He was hospitalized because he was sick, whatever. My sister didn't live that time, with the baby. And freighter, some people back. Somebody else come back. But I was single. And I said to myself, it's not a place for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I went-- at that time, I went to Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] To Hungary. And in Hungary, I met a sister [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from my brother-in-law's sister. One sister is on the-- on the-- on the [POLISH]. Sprynca. And the mother and my sister and a baby. The other one was [POLISH]. She went with-- with him to-- to-- to Hungary. And they wander to Romania. So I was in Romania with them for-- for a time in Bucharest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then it was a-- it was a talk Bricha going to-- to Israel. So I said to myself, I'm going to go too. I'm going to go next. So I went through Budapest, [BACKROUND NOISE] and I went in Budapest, Timisoara. Timisoara. There it was a big town-- a nice town in-- in Hungary. By the border to Yugoslavia was Timisoara.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you hear about this town? No. Timisoara was a big town with-- with a-- with a-- a-- tramway, you know? Tram, this here. And then I joined like a group, you know, to go to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we stayed there for days. And from there, they took us to Belgrade. Belgrade. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] In Belgrade, I stood also. They-- they-- they host us, in the-- the Judenrat, whatever it was. The-- the-- the-- what do you call it, the Jewish-- what do you call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish welfare?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not welfare. The Bricha. Bricha.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Bricha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Bricha. They-- they-- they worried about us. And they sent us from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Yugoslavia, what is the name what I said? Yugoslavia--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to-- you were in Belgrade.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. From Belgrade, we went to Italy. Belgrade. No. Belgrade, we went-- we went to a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it. To-- to Croatia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Croatia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Croatia. Agram. Ljubljana. Trieste. Then we came to Trieste. On the way-- on the way, when we came there, the English came up. And they checked. So I was so stupid. I had this letter, this Russian letter. I took the letter. We were smuggling. And I tore it away and-- and I threw it out. I should have kept it. This letter-- I can't-- I can't forgive myself. I shouldn't have threw it away. No. But at that time, I was afraid that, well, the English and they're watching. They were-- if I come from Russia today, for questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they took-- I-- we went there to-- to Mestre. To Venezia. To Trieste. From Trieste, I went to Venezia. Venezia. Mestre. And Padova. Do you hear about Padova? And there was a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed in the camp in Padova. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was in-- in-- and there, I had also a-- a guy, a Jewish guy from-- from Chrzanow. Koshitsky is-- was his name. And he had a brother in Trieste, was also name of Koshitsky was [INAUDIBLE] too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he sent for [INAUDIBLE] [? [NON-ENGLISH] ?] from there, you know? So I knew I gots a Koshitsky [LAUGHS] to rely on. And I went to look for him. But I didn't find him. But I found the family. And so I-- I-- I was in Trieste. And I was in-- in-- in Venice. And I was in Padova. And from Padova-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from Padova, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to Milan. I went to Milan. At that time, there was no opening in Israel. There was no opening. It was not-- it was in '45. There was no opening. I just-- you wandered around, you know? Where I sleep one night, I didn't sleep the other night. We were always on the go. Always on the go. I slept on-- on-- on-- on-- on [GERMAN], you know? On-- on stations and the rail stations plenty times. I washed myself in-- in-- in-- in-- in the toilets. That's how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have no home. I didn't have no-- no place where-- where to be, with whom to be. From my brother, I didn't hear, because he was still there. And so I went to Milan. I went to Milan. Not-- I went to Milan. And I was in Milan, [ITALIAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you hear about [ITALIAN]? Did you hear about in-- in the-- in the [? San Marco, ?] the place? How do you hear? Yeah? In the gallerias there. So [LAUGHS]. And-- You ended up-- from Milano, you went on to?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Milano, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went up to-- to Rome. [LAUGHS] From Rome, I organized something. I-- I-- I did something, you know? I-- I had to start. And I had-- I bought some-- some British, the fund, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Pound?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pound. Pounds. Paper pounds. And I took them to Poland. [LAUGHS] I went to Poland, but there was no connection-- there was no-- there was no way [LAUGHS] how to go to Poland. And I-- and-- and-- and I didn't sleep one night without-- before-- where I slept other night. I never. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So from there, Poland, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I sold those-- [LAUGHS] those-- those pounds, English pounds for a lot of money. Mark. German mark. They-- I got a valise, a full valise, of the-- [LAUGHS] of the German marks. And-- and-- and in [? Weiden, ?] in Germany, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had a guy.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is the same thing as the [POLISH] Rabinowitsch. [POLISH] Rabinowitsch. Shlomek Rabinowitsch. They were now in Belgium. One is in Australia and one is in Belgium. And they were dealing and wheeling. So I gave them the marks, and he gave me the money back in-- in Germany, and-- and-- and I went to Munich. Munich. Munich, I found [SIGHS] relatives in Feldafing, next to the Feldafing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay at the DP camp in Feldafing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shortly. Then I went back to Munich. And I found out-- found an apartment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I live in the apartment with a friend. This was an apartment which was requisite because the German who-- who I had an apartment, he was giving out-- he-- he was a squeak by the Germans of other Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by the end of the war, they [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] this German. So they gave me the apartment. When I-- then-- then I heard my brother come from-- from Poland. He came back. So I-- I-- I-- I looked for a way to come to us, to me. And he came to me. We joined together.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and she was at that time pregnant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was pregnant?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-in-law was pregnant. And they lived in a room. The Germans lived in one room by the beginning of the door, by the entrance. We had the rest of the apartment. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One good day, we didn't have with them peace. They called police. They-- they-- they-- they-- they-- for-- for-- for-- for-- for-- with lies that we smuggled. We did this. We did that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [SIGHS] by the end of it, they couldn't-- they couldn't do anything. They want us out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at night [SIGHS] [BACKROUND NOISE] my sister-in-law went down. She has-- she's pregnant. She felt gas. She felt gas. She goes into the kitchen, and she sees three people laying on the gas, and the gas was running.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all killed themselves. Mother, father, the one without the hand, and the daughter. The daughter was the worst. But they all killed themselves. And the gas was running. Well, it was-- was-- [SIGHS] was very dangerous. We called police. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And by searching the apartment, we found a bomb in-- in-- in the pantry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the pantry. It was in the paper. It was in the German paper. It was full of-- full of the German paper at that time. Me, my brother, and-- and my--

speaker: interviewee
text: This family placed the bomb in there that would have exploded--

speaker: interviewee
text: They placed the bomb, and they gassed themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would have killed you after--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --they gassed themselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. With-- with other words, they-- they wanted to see us dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the family's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whose name?

speaker: interviewer
text: The German family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I ask my brother. I-- I don't remember. I-- I-- I-- well, it was on [GERMAN]. [GERMAN], Munich, Steinhausen. So it is--

speaker: interviewer
text: When? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewer
text: Beginning, middle--

speaker: interviewer
text: The end of '45, beginning of '46?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say '46. '46, because but-- my brother was there. He wasn't there '45. '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you after all your roaming around stay in Munich? Did you decide to stay in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no place where to go. Where do they go? And then we opened up businesses in Munich. We had a grocery on [GERMAN] in Munich. A big-- a big business. Me with my brother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And also we imported textiles from Italy. And I went there. I was before in-- in Munich-- in-- in Milano, [ITALIAN]. And you see, I had connections. I had connections. [ITALIAN] and [ITALIAN] and-- and Trento, in Milano. I was in Firenze. I was in Bologna. I was all-- all Munich, I was there. In Rome. I was-- I was traveling there. So I had connections, and I brought in goods to-- to Germany, and we sold it there. So it was-- it was business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the kippah on your head at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The kippah on my head.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did it come back on your head?

speaker: interviewee
text: It came back, I still-- later. Later.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also. Yeah. Well, actually, till-- I guess-- I married here. And when I married, I was-- I was wearing the yarmulke. I wore the yarmulke. It was in '40-- in '52. But I married. But I wasn't-- I was till-- till '52, I was in Germany. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to synagogue in Germany? You found yourself--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What synagogue did you belong to in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] In Munich, on [GERMAN], the main synagogue. What was his name, the rabbi? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Munich. Yeah. Laserowsky. Rabbi Laserowsky. He was known. On [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you decide to come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Living in Germany was temporary. No matter how long I lived there, but it was-- it-- the mind was temporary. It was not permanent. So let's put it this way. When I had a chance to get a visa, so from time to time I got a call. So I was-- I-- one step nearer. Until the day came that they gave me-- they gave me the passports.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- but I didn't go with-- with-- with-- what do you call it? HIAS. But I went by my own. I took the from Paris. And on the way there, at the time from-- from Germany, I-- I traveled to Paris and I traveled to-- to-- to-- to-- to Italy, and I traveled to Holland. All in business. And I made out. And your brother stayed?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother stayed. My brother tended the leather business. We were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue the leather business when you came to America in '52?

speaker: interviewee
text: To America? When I came here, we had a factory there, yeah. A leather factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you start one here as well? A factory here as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a factory here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Jacob Street. You know where Jacob Street is? Now it's the-- now the-- the-- the Brooklyn Bridge is on top of it. Underneath was Cliff Street, Jacob Street, and William Street. There, we had a building. And we had-- we had a tannery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- you-- you went back and forth to Europe for--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you did it here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother kept the business there, and you kept the business--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother came after me right away. I came here in March. He came in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who sponsored you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who sponsored me? [SIGHS] I don't know even. They sponsored me. Actually, my-- my-- my affidavit was to Seattle, Washington. Seattle. [LAUGHS] I didn't know where to go. Seattle. I came here. I changed it. They sent me to Seattle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to Seattle first?

speaker: interviewee
text: No! No. I went to New York. [LAUGHS] And I changed it here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Incredible.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel at this point? As you said, you were a wandering Jew. What-- what-- what did you feel about life? What did you feel about what you wanted from life at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this point-- now?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1952. You reached America.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married and I-- I got a family, and I-- I got business [LAUGHS] and I got to tan something, you know. I was busy. Believe me, I was very busy. I-- I worked hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your wife's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Irene. Sura. Sura Ruchel, I'm sorry. Sura Ruchel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pfefferman, with a P. Double F.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here. Somebody make connections.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: --children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to name your children for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avrum [? Leibish ?] is Alan. And Rifka Mindl is Renee. And Avrum [? Leibish-- ?] and-- and-- and-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Reuven Shmuel is Richard. This is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have four grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four grandchildren, and-- and I hope more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- just give me their names, your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandchildren's, English? English? Alexandra and Maxie, Maxine. This English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish, is Zahava Eve. The other one is after their family. I don't even remember what I told you. I have it written down. [LAUGHS] It's not out of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other children is Carly and Adam-- Adam-- Adam Jake. In Jewish, it's Hannah Pesha, and he is Avraham Yisrael. This is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you belong to Young Israel here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Belong to Young Israel Hillcrest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your feelings of how you were being brought up in your religion from the background come back to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let me tell you something. This is deep and rooted. You cannot-- you cannot take it out. You cannot take it out. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it just-- just came back naturally--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you the truth now, something else. Today, this morning, I had a [YIDDISH]. You know what's a [YIDDISH]? Learning. Everyday in the morning. Saturday. So-- but I don't have a rabbi. We two, another companion of mine from Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still study--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We study together. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was from Krakow when you left when you were a young man? From Krakow, you knew him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's my age. And he was from Krakow. I was from Chrzanow. But I know-- I knew him from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have friends from your hometown here? Anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course I have. I had a book there. I have a society, the Chrzanow Society--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We meet. We have anniversaries. And we have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every-- once a year, a party. We meet. All the Chrzanow [INAUDIBLE]. If you want to-- if you want to-- if you want to come there sometimes, you're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said also that you went back to visit Auschwitz four years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you decide to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I didn't go just to Poland. But I-- I had a trip, a big trip. On the way back, my son [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] has the young son, has a publishing-- had a publishing. Now he has also a partner in something else, also Polish. And he dealt with Lufthansa. And he accumulated a travel mileage. So he gave me mileage. He gave me mileage, a lot of mileage. Yeah. I was-- I was in a lot of countries. So I went to-- to Spain. And I went to Morocco. And I went to Germany. I went to-- I went to France. And I went to Poland. And then I came back. Everything was Lufthansa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, though, Poland? Why not--

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland, I want-- I want to see [POLISH]. I want to see [POLISH]. I want to see how Poland is. What-- what-- you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think about when you were walking through Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Auschwitz? [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What shall I say about-- what-- what shall-- what word should I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe that you actually went through it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not-- it's unbelievable. It's unbelievable. It's just coincidence that I-- that I got out, and that's all it is. Because if somebody asked me, how did you come out? I ask--I tell them, if I wouldn't be here, you-- you wouldn't got-- have nobody to ask for. And that's all it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no way.There was no way of out. If you ask me-- you ask me once, did you work something else? If somebody worked in-- in-- Sonderkommando, there was no something else. There was no something else, and that's what it is. And your memories today of that time? How do you cope with daily living and those memories? What do you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- I had a lot of times [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where I had depression. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I used to go down middle of the night and walk around here and-- and-- and couldn't get dressed. And I take now Prozac. And Prozac gives me-- I-- somehow gives me dreams which I'm very happy with it. Some people don't like it. But I find it that to me it's-- it's-- it's relaxing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you spoken to people about this? Do you speak about what happened to you to other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: About the stories which I told you? I speak out. I speak out sometimes. But not-- not everything at once. You know, episodes. Episodes. Know what I mean? A lot of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: To your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My children.

speaker: interviewer
text: They know the-- most of the story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My children. My wife. My friends. The shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people are willing to listen to you and you're-- you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I tell stories of how it was. Like-- like for example, they brought people to be gassed. They-- they knew the moment what's going to happen. They realize. There-- there's-- there's no way of-- of not-- not realizing. So they-- they-- they scream Shema Yisrael.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the other side was this SS man. He started laughing. 'Shema [? Yitzraim! ?] Shema [? Yitzraim!' ?] You know what I mean? Shema Yisrael, Shema [? Yitzraim. ?] To them was Shema [? Yitzraim. ?] Those-- those kind of-- of-- of-- of words, there was-- there was no resent-- resentment.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no resentment. There was no resentment. At least not when-- when I-- among those-- those which I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: You actually heard the-- you-- did you see the people walking into the gas chambers? Did you see them walking in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw them taking off the clothes. I wasn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on top of them. But I-- I-- I heard the-- the-- the-- the dogs barking, and they chased them. First of all, they went there. And then it was full, and they could-- with no place to go. Then start the-- the-- the-- the dogs, the-- the rest of it, you know? Until it was full, full. Close the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear them screaming inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could be heard. Could be heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they came out, and then it was up to you-- it went-- they went to the-- the-- the bodies afterwards went to the mortuary?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- after the gassing, there was a commander which was the [? schleppers. ?] You know? They pulled by the hand or by the legs. And they put on masks, and they ventilated. Because it was impossible to be there. And they cleaned it, because everybody-- everybody was soiled. This is-- this is-- this is a terrible death. It's a terrible death. One steps on the other. One-- one just-- the place inside looked-- there was no place to-- to sit down. Everybody was one on top of the other. And some of them held themselves other. You had to force it to separate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anger. Do you live with anger today about what people did to you personally?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not calm. I'm not calm. But I try my best.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is it that gives you relief? And I-- I know that you said medication helps you. What are other things that have helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Reading.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things do you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things do you enjoy reading?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of thing? History. Jewish reading. And I love Jewish reading in-- in Tanach. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I read.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I'd to have you answer one more question, and that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the question is why you decided to do this tape for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told you before, I made already a tape, and I made another tape. But I overheard in this neighborhood somebody made a tape. So I said, why not me? I have a story like anybody else. If-- if they have tapes where I-- I know what-- where they were. So I have-- I have more stories to tell than they do. This-- this made me call.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Because your stories--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I--

speaker: interviewer
text: --are important.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I called once, it was already time back. I didn't have no answer from you. But it came months, months, months went by. And then a call came to me back. So I told them, I'm ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you. I'm happy you were patient and you waited.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you we appreciate--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you telling us your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I appreciate you too. I appreciate you too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you.
